Obie Fernandez
@obie
It Might Be Time to Say Goodbye to HTML Inputs
I replaced every "Create" and "Edit" button in my web app with a copy-paste prompt for Claude Code. And it works better than forms ever did.

The backstory

Empirium is an experiment-tracking system I built for ZAR. It replaces Linear. Teams use it to manage assumptions, run experiments, record learnings, track metrics. The kind of app that would normally be drowning in text-entry forms. Multi-step wizards. Nested evidence fields. Comment dialogs. Learning capture modals.
I built none of that.
My first version had forms (made in a Ralph loop). They sucked. So in a fit of ebullient audacity, I deleted all the new and edit templates and replaced the buttons that used to lead to them with small prompt popup affordances. You click them, and a dialog with a short command you can paste into Claude Code pops up. Claude calls the right MCP tool, the database updates, and Turbo Streams refresh the page in real time.
My web interface is now almost purely a read layer. Dashboards, detail pages, evidence timelines. The only interactive bits left are navigation, filtering, some status toggles, search, and profile settings. Every other write operation goes through MCP.

Really?

Yeah. Everyone at ZAR uses Claude Code. All day, every day, for everything. That's why this works.
We're living in 2028. The rest of you will get there eventually. Probably.

Laziness, Impatience, and Hubris

Complex web apps are expensive to build well. Lots of design time, good judgment, taste, iteration. Linear is the pinnacle of the form—must have taken tens of thousands of hours to build that UX.
But giving Claude Code MCP access to your API is easy, and it handles complicated structured input perfectly. Master-detail records, nested learnings, conditional fields, whatever. Several forms I was contemplating for Empirium were going to be multi-section beasts with radio groups, conditional visibility, and validation UX that takes forever to get right.
But won't my users get frustrated? What hubris!
Lazy, impatient, and hubristic. Which according to Larry Wall is what makes me a great programmer. 🤓

The prompt bubble

What makes the UX somewhat intuitive despite being weird is a small reusable UI element I call the prompt bubble. It renders a small icon that opens a native dialog with a pre-composed command. One click copies it to clipboard.
Note the MCP PROMPT dialog
The icons follow a convention: plus for create, pencil for edit, terminal for general MCP actions, chat for comments, arrow-right for transitions.
The prompts are imperative with just enough context—entity IDs, natural keys, names, URLs. Enough that Claude Code finds the right resource without follow-up questions. The user copies the prompt, finishes it in their terminal. If they don't supply required attributes, Claude helps them figure it out.
The conversation IS the user experience.

Caveats

I think this approach is actually best suited for internal teams where everyone runs Claude Code (or something like it). This is not a pattern for consumer apps, unless you're targeting consumers with
Claws
.
The prompt bubbles are kind of silly and won't get used much once users understand the modality. But their presence matters more than you'd think. Without them, new users would be confused. With them, it's immediately: "Oh, okay. I see what you did there."
You'll probably still need some forms—onboarding, ceremony-type interactions where clicking through IS the experience. For instance, I kept normal forms for profile and settings.

Two more practical tips

First: give them an MCP setup page. Put it in your main navigation. Clear instructions, inline API key generation. Don't make them hunt for it.
Second: make your views update in real time. Turbo Streams with model callbacks are what make the read-only UI feel alive. Without broadcasts, you're telling people to refresh their browser, and that kills the whole thing.

Five commits on a Friday night

The entire transformation was five commits. The big rewrite, prompt bubble polish, z-index fixes (always z-index), cleaning up dead form code. Testing. Deployed. One of my favorite things about these times is how brazenly cocky you can be with design decisions like this. Because the cost is minimal.
What a time to be alive.
Even my dog can code these days. Admittedly he's pretty smart.
